title: Parts Registries — From iGEM to BIOFAB

lines.text: Section eleven. Parts registries — from iGEM to professional infrastructure.
We have already touched on the Registry of Standard Biological Parts when discussing BioBricks, and on the iGEM connection in chapter two.
But the foundational-technologies framing deserves a closer look at where parts registries are going.
The original iGEM parts registry, at parts.igem.org, was the first large-scale attempt to give synthetic biology a shared, community-maintained catalogue of standard parts.
It is hosted in the United States by a dedicated team, and it operates roughly as a wiki — iGEM teams submit DNA sequence information and characterisation data for the parts they create or reuse.
Over years of accumulated submissions, the registry now catalogues several thousand parts.
This is an enormous resource — without iGEM and its registry, synthetic biology as a teaching subject would simply not exist.
But the registry's limitations are real, and the authors do not soft-pedal them.
Because iGEM is primarily an undergraduate competition, the focus of submitting teams has historically been quantity rather than depth of characterisation.
Parts are deposited with sequence information and a description of intended function, but often without rigorous quantitative measurements.
Parts may not always work as described.
Submission validation is minimal.
Data formats are inconsistent across submissions.
Metadata about how parts were measured is often absent.
For a competition, this is fine.
For a professional engineering discipline that wants to design with confidence, it is not enough.
The community's response has been to build professional-grade registries alongside the iGEM one — registries committed to rigorous characterisation, standardised data formats, and reliable function.
Two prominent examples.
Many commercial synthetic biology companies maintain closed-source internal registries with deep characterisation of their own proprietary parts.
And open-source equivalents are emerging.
The most prominent open professional registry is the BIOFAB — the International Open Facility Advancing Biotechnology — founded in 2009 in the United States with public funding.
BIOFAB's mission was to design and rigorously characterise hundreds of high-quality biological parts and make them publicly available in a registry that is, by design, professional-grade.
Since 2013 their parts have been available in a public-facing registry.

Critically, BIOFAB committed to defining the measurement standards for the parts themselves — what data must be collected, in what units, with what precision — so that downstream design tools and modelling software can ingest BIOFAB parts data programmatically.

BIOFAB stores its parts data in a format compatible with SBOL, the Synthetic Biology Open Language, which is the field's emerging standard for the machine-readable representation of biological designs.
Both the BioBricks Registry and BIOFAB are committed to being open source, and the broader politics of open-source biology gets a fuller treatment in chapter nine.

For our foundational-technologies purposes, here is the takeaway: parts registries are essential infrastructure, the community is in the middle of an evolution from the somewhat-loose iGEM registry to professional-grade registries with stronger characterisation standards, and the trend is clearly toward machine-readable formats that let parts data flow seamlessly between registry, model, and design tool.

If you want to know which specific parts to use for a synthetic biology project today, you will probably end up consulting both iGEM and BIOFAB-style registries, and the tooling to do so cleanly is itself an active area of development.
